title: Little Sloan Leaning Bookshelf
summary: A wall-mounted ladder frame at a 10° lean holds three staggered plywood boxes — the angle is the whole design, not a decoration on top of a square shelf.
description:
The leaning frame isn't cosmetic — every frame piece is cut to a matching 10° angle on both ends, parallel to each other, which is what lets the whole ladder lean back convincingly instead of just looking like a crooked square shelf. Cut the frame pieces as a matched set before you touch the boxes, and don't mix a piece from one angle setting with a piece from another; a few degrees of drift between pieces is very visible on a leaning silhouette.

The three storage boxes are sized differently on purpose — small, medium, large, stepping up as they go down the ladder — and this is a mounted piece, not free-standing: the frame anchors to wall studs and carries real load, so don't skip the stud-mounting step or treat it as optional the way you might on a floor shelf.

tools.note: Every frame piece needs a matching 10° angle on both ends — set it once and cut the whole set.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4"
materials.note: At least 15-1/2" x 31" — can substitute MDF or a project panel.

materials.name: Lag screws, 3" or longer
materials.note: Anchors the frame to wall studs.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 10° off square, ends parallel, long-point-to-short-point measurement.
Both ends cut at 10° off square, ends parallel, long-point-to-short-point measurement.
Both ends cut at 10° off square, ends parallel.
Both ends cut at 10° off square, ends parallel.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body:
Set the miter saw to 10° and cut every angled frame piece in one sitting without touching the setting — mixing a piece cut at one setting with one cut at another is very visible on a leaning silhouette. From the 1x3: two long frame legs at 57-3/8", two medium legs at 29-3/4", two upper rungs at 11-3/8" and two lower rungs at 11-1/8", all with both ends at 10° and the two ends of each piece parallel, measured long point to short point. Then square-cut the 1x3 bottom spacer at 15", the top shelf front at 16-1/2" and two top shelf sides at 8". The three 1x3 boards cover all of that — 266-3/4" of the 288" supplied, with one long leg and one medium leg fitting each of the first two boards.

The 2x2 top spacer is the whole 15" piece as supplied. From the 1x6: two medium box ends at 10" and two front and back pieces at 16-1/2". From the 1x8: two large box ends at 12-1/2" and two front and back pieces at 16-1/2". From the 3/4" plywood, three box bottoms all 15" wide and 8", 10" and 12-1/2" long — that is 30-1/2" of the 31" minimum panel the materials call for, so lay all three out before the first cut.

steps.title: Build the two leg sets as mirror images
steps.body:
Each leg set is one 57-3/8" long leg and one 29-3/4" medium leg held apart by two rungs — the 11-3/8" upper and the 11-1/8" lower. Build the two sets as mirror images, not identical copies, or the finished frame will lean sideways as well as backwards. Keep all the 10° cuts running the same way within each set so the legs stay parallel to one another.

Before anything is fastened permanently, stand a box between the rungs and check the gap clears it easily. Moving a rung now is a two-minute job; after the frame is assembled it is a rebuild, and the plan does not dimension the rung positions, so this dry check is the only thing setting them. Join with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws, drilled on the faces that will end up against the wall.

steps.title: Join the frame with top and bottom
steps.body:
Stand the two leg sets 15" apart and join them with the 15" 2x2 at the top and the 15" 1x3 at the bottom, fastening with 1-1/4" pocket screws.

That makes the frame 16-1/2" wide over the outside faces of the two 3/4" legs — the same 16-1/2" as every box front and back, so the boxes will finish flush with the frame sides. If your frame comes out wider or narrower than that, fix it before you build a box to it. Lay the assembled frame on a flat floor and check it does not rock: with a 10° lean only the leg tops and the feet touch, so a twist here shows up later as a shelf that will not sit tight to the wall.

steps.title: Build the shelf and boxes
steps.body:
All three boxes are 16-1/2" wide to match the frame, and they step up in both depth and height as they go down the ladder — that stagger is the design, not a coincidence. The top shelf is shallowest: a 15" x 8" plywood bottom with a 16-1/2" 1x3 front and two 8" 1x3 sides. The medium box is 1x6 — a 15" x 10" bottom, two 10" ends and two 16-1/2" front and back pieces. The large box is 1x8 with a 15" x 12-1/2" bottom, two 12-1/2" ends and two 16-1/2" front and back pieces.

Fasten with 1-1/4" finish nails or with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws. Check each box across the diagonals before moving on — a racked box will not slide into a frame whose gaps you already set to fit a square one.

steps.title: Mount the frame and add the shelf and boxes
steps.body:
Find the studs before you drill anything. This is a wall-carried piece, not a floor shelf, and the lag screws are the only thing holding it up — drywall anchors are not an option here. The frame's legs sit 15" apart on the inside, which puts their centres about 15-3/4" apart: close to the usual 16" stud spacing but not the same, so locate your studs first and be ready to shift the frame or add a mounting cleat rather than assuming they line up.

Drill clearance holes through the legs with the drill-bit-set and drive the lag screws home into solid framing. With the frame secure, slide each box into its bay — smallest at the top, largest at the bottom — and screw through the frame legs into the box sides. Sand every edge and finish.

images.alt: Wall-mounted leaning bookshelf with a 10-degree angled frame and three staggered storage boxes
